item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article comes from here and is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Some of this feedback may no longer be relevant due to the author updating the article*
I like this SCP and will upvote, but I don't understand why they would redact the result of improper use of the sword entirely.

Also this is more a personal thing, why is the second addendum information the Foundation would have had first after getting the object in custody and why didn't they question Tony about the hands and sword?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this SCP idea, I really do. But the problem that stood out to me the most is the fact that there is no storyline to this SCP article. Storylines aren't required but they can make any article significantly more interesting. For this maybe you could focus on SCP-4483's discovery and then how it was discovered and why, but I recommend having a story that will leave readers with questions and will keep them guessing of what might happen next.

Still got a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Replacing the redaction with some variation of the phrase “was left as an animate pile of bowels” would really help this imo.

You’ve hit on something creepy with the trapped-in-part-of-your-own-body-as-horror concept, but this errs a little on the side of minimalism and doesn’t flesh out enough about the effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess what I was trying to do was keep the article more concise. Instead of building a mystery, I was trying to lean more into the horror of the idea of being trapped in a piece of your own body, most of which I tried to develop in the Addendum. I see what you're saying about the testing logs - they are the first non-description piece you really get to - but maybe they could actually be removed, allowing the reader a quicker transition into the rest of the addendum?

As for your second point (the redacted addendum) the reason I did that was because I felt it would work better to let the reader's mind fill in the blank rather than just say "he was left as a pile of animate bowels."

Also, yeah, i had a bit of trouble with the penultimate addendum. But it's not meant to be a standalone addendum as much as it's meant to tie in with the final one - if you look at it, it essentially means that, despite the horrific state the first batch of -1s were recovered in, they actually wouldn't be eligible for euthanization since they are technically capable of rudimentary communication.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda like the idea of the SCP here: my -1 was more for the execution. Specifically, my big issue is that the real meat of this article is, I guess, the testing logs and they really don't reveal much to the reader beyond how the Foundation figured out how it works; and even then it feels very much like the examples provided wouldn't be sufficient. Like, the way Campbell figures it out feels like it only works if you already know how it works? But as a second test, all it's really established is "the majority of the body remains alive".

This wouldn't be a fatal flaw, except it's followed by an Addendum of the SCP being used where the one actually interesting thing that happens is redacted instead of having some really fun, vivid imagery; and then an Addendum featuring relatively generic pleas for help.

I feel like this would work better if the article was written in a lot more of a provisional state: where the description admits the Foundation doesn't exactly know what the sword does so in turn a reader doesn't exactly know what the sword does; and then you can have a bunch of testing and like gradually reveal it so the reader can piece together what it does as the Foundation does too. Or alternatively: if the reader knows exactly what the sword does and is forced to watch in horror as the Foundation tests hundreds of things in a desperate attempt to figure it out.

item.title: Cut off the head
item.content:encoded:
An idea I started working on back in April last year. Just got around to finishing it. It's based on the weird fact that when referring to decapitation, people always refer to it as "cutting off the head", although from the perspective of the person being decapitated, now matter how brief, it would really probably seem more like "cutting off the body".

EDIT: as noted below, the image comes from Wikimedia Commons and is in the public domain.
